text: Nearly a century after its discovery, Fischer–Tropsch synthesis (FTS) remains a focal point of scientific debate and industrial importance. This review examines the recent shift toward a molecular-level understanding of the process, driven by the integration of surface science, density functional theory (DFT), and microkinetic modeling. For cobalt-based catalysts, significant progress has been made in characterizing the structure of nanoparticles and the formation of stable CO overlayers. Modern simulations and experimental data are converging on a consensus regarding active sites—specifically the role of terraced surfaces versus step-edges—and the debate between carbide and CO-insertion mechanisms. In contrast, iron-based catalysts present a more complex challenge due to the dynamic evolution of multiple oxide and carbide phases under reaction conditions. While current research has begun to clarify the stability of these phases and the influence of chemical promoters, a definitive molecular "movie" of the active site remains incomplete. Finally, the paper highlights the emerging relevance of CO2-FTS as a sustainable pathway for producing long-chain hydrocarbons, discussing how existing molecular insights into CO hydrogenation can be adapted to advance carbon capture and utilization technologies.
